paper.title: Tokencake: A KV-Cache-centric Serving Framework for LLM-based Multi-Agent Applications

presentation_thesis: Tokencake argues that multi-agent LLM serving should treat KV cache as a shared workflow resource across both time and space: proactive offload and prefetch exploit tool stalls, while critical-path-aware reservation prevents agent interference.

problem_framing.label: Workload shift
problem_framing.value: Multi-agent applications combine agent-agent dependencies with long external function calls, so KV-cache usage is no longer a simple per-request lifecycle.

problem_framing.label: Space contention
problem_framing.value: A non-critical agent can evict a critical-path agent, forcing context recomputation and stalling the entire workflow.

problem_framing.label: Time underutilization
problem_framing.value: During a function call, an agent's KV cache sits idle on the GPU; the paper reports peaks where 18.5% of the used KV-cache pool is occupied by stalled agents.

problem_framing.label: Prior-work gap
problem_framing.value: Compute-centric agent schedulers ignore GPU memory state, while memory-centric KV-cache systems remain agent-agnostic and reactive.

claims.statement: Agentic multi-agent workloads expose two distinct KV-cache failures: critical-path contention in space and idle-cache underutilization in time.
evidence: 18.5% idle used KV cache at peak

claims.statement: Tokencake's core contribution is to co-optimize KV-cache management across both dimensions instead of treating scheduling and memory management as separate problems.
The Time Scheduler uses function-call awareness to proactively offload stalled agents and predictively upload their KV cache before resumption.
The Space Scheduler protects critical agents through hybrid-priority scoring and dynamic memory partitioning rather than FIFO allocation.
Co-optimizing the two schedulers improves loaded-system performance, cutting end-to-end latency by over 47.06% and raising effective GPU KV utilization by up to 16.9% versus vLLM.
The offload policy is practical because transfer is far cheaper than recomputation, and Tokencake's buffering and reservation optimizations keep upload overhead in the millisecond range.

mechanisms.label: Frontend graph plus metadata
mechanisms.detail: Users describe the agent DAG and function-call nodes, including predicted tool time, so the runtime can reason about dependencies and stall windows.

mechanisms.label: Time Scheduler
mechanisms.detail: Static graph analysis finds LLM -> tool -> LLM patterns; runtime call_start and call_finish events drive benefit-based offload and predictive upload.

mechanisms.label: Space Scheduler
mechanisms.detail: The scheduler designates the top critical_ratio of agent types as critical using hybrid priority, then splits GPU KV memory into shared and reserved pools that adapt to memory pressure.

mechanisms.label: Offload optimizations
mechanisms.detail: CPU block buffering and gradual GPU block reservation keep frequent offload and upload operations cheap enough to use proactively.

evaluation_setup.label: Applications
evaluation_setup.value: Code-Writer and Deep Research represent tool-heavy, dependency-rich multi-agent workloads.

evaluation_setup.label: Baselines
evaluation_setup.value: vLLM and LightLLM isolate the effect of Tokencake's agent-aware memory policies because neither baseline includes proactive offloading or predictive uploading.

evaluation_setup.label: Metrics
evaluation_setup.value: End-to-end latency, GPU KV-cache utilization, agent latency, abnormal-agent count, and offload or recompute microbenchmarks.

evaluation_setup.label: Load model
evaluation_setup.value: Request arrivals follow a Poisson process with comparisons reported across increasing QPS.

quantitative_anchors.label: Latency improvement
quantitative_anchors.value: over 47.06% lower end-to-end latency versus vLLM at 1.0 QPS

quantitative_anchors.label: KV utilization
quantitative_anchors.value: up to 16.9% higher effective GPU KV-cache utilization

quantitative_anchors.label: Idle cache peak
quantitative_anchors.value: 18.5% of used KV cache occupied by stalled agents at peak

quantitative_anchors.label: Transfer versus recompute
quantitative_anchors.value: 4096 blocks: about 60 ms transfer versus nearly 9,000 ms recomputation

quantitative_anchors.label: Upload overhead mitigation
quantitative_anchors.value: 5120-block upload drops from 15,163 ms to 4.4 ms with Tokencake's optimizations

quantitative_anchors.label: Critical-path metric
quantitative_anchors.value: abnormal agent means execution time above 1.5x the type average

support.detail: 18.5% idle used KV cache at peak
Frontend API, Space Scheduler, and Time Scheduler cooperate on one graph.
Tokencake is proactive and function-call-aware, unlike reactive baselines.
Hybrid priority plus pressure-aware reservation protects critical agents.
47.06% latency reduction and 16.9% higher utilization under load.
Transfer stays far cheaper than recomputation once Tokencake's optimizations are applied.

limitations.label: Prediction model
limitations.value: Tool-time prediction is deliberately simple; richer features such as call arguments are left to future work.

limitations.label: Single-GPU scope
limitations.value: The evaluation is limited to one GPU, so distributed KV-cache placement remains open.

limitations.label: Benchmark breadth
limitations.value: The paper evaluates two representative multi-agent applications rather than a broad public benchmark suite.

notes: Table 2 is useful background, but the deck can cover the prior-work gap with concise text boxes instead of a dense table crop.
